speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. December 11, 1995. We're interviewing Mendel Flaster in San Diego, in English. We left off where you were in Birkenau. How did you get from there to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks, you know, and they took from Birkenau to Auschwitz but everything they had trucks that took us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got Auschwitz, they put me over there to sort the clothes for the people that went into the crematoriums. When I see what they are doing with the people, when I seen the woman having the children clenched to their breasts, and they're going in with lies-- they are telling them where they going in over there to take a shower. They never came out alive. I couldn't stand this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went into a-- over there, is place where they called it the [NON-ENGLISH], and I asked them to send me away, another place. But they sent me to Buna Auschwitz. How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz I was only a few days. I couldn't stand it very long there. Only a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come they would let you move to somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I begged them. And there was over there one of the Germans, you know, that there was in the camp. That was the Germans also, with the-- with the green wrinkle. You know they had the different colors. You know-- the numbers what we had, as we had the Star of David, they had, you know, other kind of signets. You know, and there was different colors. His was green. That means he was a thief or something. You know, they had black, green, red, whatever, you know, different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me to Buna. How do you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also by truck. And in Buna, they went there to send us in and there was not me alone. There was quite a few people. We came in over there to Barrack 34. And that was also quarantined. We stayed over there for eight days in the quarantine. When they sent us out to work from the quarantine, they sent us outside the camp. Because Buna was a very big factory. It was the-- Hermann Goering's Werke-- that mean Hermann Goering could-- you know, this was his factory. And over there, everything was done in that factory. Thousands and thousands, tens of thousands of people working in that factory. All kind nationalities-- they were working in that factory. But we had to work-- the first days, we had to go out to outside, and we had the shoes, only the wooden shoes. And that was already the first snow come down, and the-- and when you were walking, the snow got stuck to the-- and this was moving around. We didn't have any socks, we didn't have anything to wrap our feet. And that wood was rubbing on the feet to the bare bone. It was very painful. But we had to throw away the shoes and just walk barefoot in the snow. We came over there to work. It was so cold, the SS made a fire, and they allowed everybody to go to the fire for a minute or two to warm up, and to go back to work. But I know it-- at the exactly that if we will go warm up and go back, this is even worse. We had to work as hard as we could in order to circulate the blood, and not to get frozen. I told all my friends not to go to the fire, because that is murder. People what they didn't listen. They went to the fire, they thought that-- they got sick the same day. They got sick, they came down with a-- a cold, put them in the hospital, and many of them didn't come out alive. Matter of fact, me, you know because I didn't-- I was rubbing my feet got frozen, the toes-- I rub them with the snow, the-- the toes. But they got frozen anyway. I have still problems with my feet today. And from our people, what we came to Birkenau Auschwitz, 5,500 plus. Half were killed right away. The doctor took us to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the other half, guess how many people was alive? We walked out from-- from Birkenau, I mean from Buna Auschwitz, only maybe about a dozen people alive from all that. And that number, if you will find a dozen people, that's a lot. Where did you sleep when you were in Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had barracks. The barracks were the-- very, very good. There were also barracks with the bunk beds, but two of them. Not the three, just two. And it was-- that alone in the Birkenau Auschwitz, was good, because there was enough water to-- to wash up, there was lavatories, and that was OK. But all of rest of it, was not OK. The food was not OK. The work was too hard. And the-- the conditions, they were-- the way that they were treating you is very unhumane. In Buna-- if we-- if we finished the quarantine, they give us to another barrack, you know, and another work. When I did work in a factory, what I cut my fingers, and later, when I went to the hospital, or sort of, I cut the fingers-- it was around 1 o'clock in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cut them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was working on a machine where I was tighten up, you know, cutting iron. And that was right after the lunch break. SS man came in the back, but I didn't even see him, and he hit me over the head with a stick. And I got excited. I forgot to tighten up the iron, and let the machine in motion, and it cut my fingers. But that's happened around 1 o'clock, but after 1 o'clock that was bleeding till about 9 o'clock in the evening where, in the hospital, a doctor came in what they sewed me up. What he operated and sewed it up. All the time was bleeding. But my luck was that this was in the wintertime. It was cold. And when it's in the wintertime, the blood doesn't flow so rapidly as in the summer. If this had been the summer, I would bleed to death. But was in the winter, and it was very cold, very frozen, so it was just drip, drip, drip, you know, till 9 o'clock in the evening. And look, the luck. When I came in right in the barrack over there in the hospital after the operation, I right away, over there were people more sicker than I was. So I right away start helping them. At night they had to go down to the toilet-- they had to-- to give a little bit water, thing like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down and I helped them. And that, the block elder-- the-- the oldest in the barrack, he got to know in this, that I am helping other people. He like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Dr. Mengele came to the hospital where he emptied the hospital twice a week-- Monday and Friday, he came in and took all the people to the crematoriums. He didn't let people lay in that room. I was six weeks over there. But luckily, every time he came my card was pulled out and he said that I am a helper here, that I helping them. And he took me and he saved my life. Six weeks I was in the hospital because that didn't heal. You see, if that didn't heal, you know, I was there for six weeks and it saved my life. After that, I came to the-- after the six weeks, I came to the work. I worked over there in a different barrack, in barrack nine. And I-- I slept bad night, but I worked at the [NON-ENGLISH], this is where-- we were shoveling coals to the oven were making steam for the factories to work. It was hard work sometimes, and not so hard. When the wagons come we had to unload the-- the train, you know, this was on a time limit where it was very hard work. But later we could relax and was all right. I met over there the kapo, and the-- and the foreman, and I did a lot of good things in there. There's so many things that I want to jump over this, and a little bit further. Because even over there, in Buna Auschwitz, I was able to do things what it is just unheard of. But I did it, and for that, what I made friends everywhere I was. In every place I was, I made myself friends to the point where they were ready to give their life for me. But I was ready to give my life at any time for somebody else. And that's what meant a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: One time, well, you know, we were-- a foreman, his name was Valvo Hirke, he was from Lodz. Very nice guy. He was making business with the civilian French people, whatever French people working with us. But he give them, you know, dresses, and sort of-- from the camp also taken out. And they brought, you know, cigarettes, bread, salami, you know-- things like this, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to do things, you see, in there. And I help them to take it out, and I got a little bit of there, a little bit there, a little bit of soup-- and you know. And that's what, you know, was, you know, to help. You know, you help somebody else, but at the same token, you help yourself, too. Well, we did this for quite some times. One time, you know, was [INAUDIBLE], looked over, have something. It's a very long story. I like to go to where we went to from Buna, they evacuated us. We had to walk a day and a night, and another half a night to Gleiwitz, we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944, late in the summer when the snow was already down, it was already cold. Was walking in the snow, was like October, November. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And who didn't was able to walk, the SS right away shot them right in front of our eyes. Where, if you just went down to sit down, not to rest, SS men came and shot him. Didn't ask him if he can get up or what-- just got shot and left him over there in the snow. When we come to Gleiwitz, to Gleiwitz II, we stayed over there for three days. And they put us on the wagons, on open wagons what they took us to Buchenwald. The train was traveling, was standing more than traveling, because there was such a traffic that we were just on the train and on the side standing. In the meantime, other trains are coming up and down, up and down.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several hundred-- several hundred people. What this-- several were wagons, you know-- I couldn't imagine how many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women and men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In that train, was only men, because only the men-- I don't know what's happened to the woman, but the way in Buna Auschwitz were women too, but I don't know what's happened to them, where they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train. We didn't have anything to eat for days. Nothing to drink. The only thing what we've got is, it was open wagons-- if the snow fall at night, what we could get the snow together a little bit and take and wet our mouths from-- with the snow. But that's the only thing we got for like 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let you out to go to the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had over there the corners, what you did, what you did it. It was a very unsanitary thing. And they-- if somebody needed number two, it was little windows, you know, what we would pick them up and they stick out the behind. Yeah, number one we went inside. But number two, we picked them up and they stick out the behind and let it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, the funny thing is, what is not so funny, but many times, the SS were standing on every wagon. [LAUGHS]. If the-- if the wind was in the right direction-- [LAUGHS] You know? Of course we heard, [SPEAKING GERMAN] and things like this, but we know why. You know? But that's-- you know, we couldn't help it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When we came to Buchenwald, they put us in-- in the barracks, you know. And there was seven people had to sleep on one bunk bed. We had to sleep like sardines, you know, packed in the thing. That the way we're packed in Buchenwald. If at night, somebody wanted to turn because you got tired to sleep on one side, he had to wake everybody and everybody had to turn in order to-- that way what we slept. That was three bunk beds on top of one on the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the train ride you ended up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After train ride we were in Buchenwald. And in Buchenwald, I was a few weeks. They took us to Weimar to work over there, to clean the streets. Because at that time, Weimar was already in bombardment, you know, with the Allies' planes-- the Allies planes bombarded the cities. So Weimar was close to Buchenwald, but they took us on the trucks over there. Every day, we had to go to Weimar to clean the streets. After that, they-- they ask who is a professional man, what he will come on a place, but he will be sent out to another camp to work. So I was a professional, but they needed for some professionals like mechanics and things like this. So I said I'm a mechanic. I know about mechanic to do like, I know to go to the moon. You know, but I said I'm a mechanic. So they showed me some screwdrivers, and things, and I said, oh, yes-- in Jewish, I was telling them, you're doing this. You're doing this with this. He said, you know. OK, you go. The funny thing is, you know I laugh now about this, but there's such a tremendous thing. When we came over there with the other camp, and we had to be the mechanics that are really professional people, they give us a shovel with a pick, you know, to go to dig over there the places, you know, to make a road. They need professional people to that-- to make roads. So they give us a shovel with a pick, you know, to dig it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after Buchenwald-- Holzminden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks. They put us on a truck to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Buchenwald before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald I was about two months, or maybe about 10 weeks or something like this I was in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Holzminden.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. That was, I said, in Germany. Over there we had to build roads. But the thing is that there were the big generals sitting, you know, and interviewing everybody, you know, what are mechanics and things. And you had to be a really professional, you know? And they come over there they give you a shovel with a pick axe, you know, to make roads. We worked over there for several weeks. Later, this-- right away, they get us to stop the work, put us in another camp. And the other camp when we came was Drutte. And we came over there and that camp was packed already to move, because the Allied soldiers were close. They didn't want to get the people who were there, so we stayed over there for a few days. And again, put us on a train. The train-- that train was very, very long. It was at least-- not to exaggerate-- but five, six, locomotives to pull that train. Because that took people from all the camps, you know, what they had to evacuate because the Allied soldiers on the way were closing up, you know, and they didn't want to leave us, you know, for them. So they-- they put us on a train, and the train had to go from there to Bergen-Belsen. But the train was standing for days on the side, and we were in open wagons. Again, we didn't have nothing what to eat for days, for weeks. The train was-- I don't-- can't imagine how long it took, you know, the train to go from one place to another, till we came to Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the-- on the train, what would happen if someone died in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The body stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the thing is, what I failed to tell you, it was so crowded in that train, if somebody sit down on the train, couldn't get up anymore-- was trampled to death. And as-- so you're standing up for days in and days out, days and nights. You're getting tired. You have no choice. You're falling down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's good that I had friends, you know, what that took me, put me in a corner, and they stand around, you know, make like a fence that nobody will step on me. But I could rest a few hours. Later somebody else sit down, and with-- and so that way, what we changed. What we saved our lives for each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just unbearable to stand for so many hours, so many days-- and without food. You know, it is understandable that people falling down. If you fall down-- you know the-- he didn't get up anymore. And it was nobody's fault, because we were so tight, but we could not help it. When they opened the wagons every time, everywhere it came, about 40% of people were dead. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this train, when finally we came to a Celle-- it was a city not far from Hanover in Germany. The train stopped over there, and before you know, the American planes with the English planes-- all Allied planes-- came and start bombardment the train, from the last wagon to the first locomotive. The whole train. We start jumping out from the train, because here people were-- were killed. I see that here, you know, the friend is bleeding and he's laying down and here, the, you know, people-- so we jumped out. The SS, were shooting to us, but we try to run away. But another five minutes, the plane is coming back. And again, they start bombarding. Finally, I was able to run away and hide in the bushes over there. The planes came back the third time, but in the meantime, in that train was three wagons of bread-- with all kind of food. But they didn't give it to us. And we were hungry. For the-- like 10 days or more, we were on the train, we didn't have nothing what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ran to the train and we grabbed a couple loaves of bread and we ran away. When I ran back, when the planes came and started bombardment, I got wounded over there. I got a bomb split, what I have here, see the hole in my shoulders.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even know it. I didn't felt it. Nothing. But to me, was just to catch that piece of bread, because we were hungry. And I brought the bread, and the other my friends did also, and we divided. You know, we kept at whatever, you know. And-- and we ate a little bit, and the rest of we kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the meantime, a friend tells me, Meny, what's the matter? You are all bloody in the back. I said, I don't know. What do I have? And I start moving around, you know? Then I felt pain. So I took off the jacket, and they tell me you are all bloody. You have a hole in the things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we went in over there, where we were hiding, went into a German house over there. And there were two old ladies and I ask them to let us be in the cellar, you know, until the bombardment will go through and the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let us be in the cellar, and they seen I am all bloody. So they told me, don't go to the Red Cross over there to the main station, because there over there are the Hitlerjungend. You know what that Hitlerjungend is? Hitlerjungend, this is the little boys what they were-- for Hitler. Little boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were with guns. They were like gestapo. They were worse than the gestapo. When they seen any one of us, they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us, don't go over there, but go a little bit further this way. And over there is a little Red Cross, what there's only one nurse over there. And she will help you over there. You will be safe. So I sat in the cellar with my friends over there for quite some time. We ask a little bit of water, you know, what we ate a piece of bread with the water. And later I went over there, and there cleaned me up, and they give me a bandage, you know, what to help me with that. And I came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back, the SS rounded us around, and we-- took us all and they held us through the day. The next day we had all to march into Bergen-Belsen. In Bergen-Belsen, you come in, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] something is as hard to explain what that camp was. That camp was the last of the last and the worst of the worst. Dead people all over. The barracks inside were so piled up, you didn't have any room in the barracks to be. You had to be outside. And outside you had to move the bodies away to make room for you to sit down that you can sleep outside. No food. Nothing. No water. It was over there, the water dirty, but we took it and we drank that. But it was absolutely murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already April, or end of March. End of March in 1945. We stayed in that camp, well, I would say at least about five weeks, till the evacuation-- the liberation came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the day you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you. First, in that I camp we were sick because we were poisoned in that camp. As we didn't have nothing what to eat, only that bread what I told you what we got from the train, what we ate. But before and after we didn't got nothing to eat. Everybody was hungry. They brought in truck loads of bread, and everybody had to get a piece of bread like this. That bread was with ground glass, poisoned. We ate. I remember still today, when I ate that bread, that I felt in the teeth, you know, that glass. But we were hungry. We ate. And that bread killed so many people, because it cut the-- you know, the inside, you know, the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, cut-- the glass. And people got diarrhea later, and dying in the thousands. When the day came, April the 15, early morning, when the first two Jeeps rolled in in the camp, nine soldiers came. And they came down and we were crawling to them, you know, and kissing their hand, because we could see already the liberation.
